title: Vote Service Container Build and Artifact Registry Delivery
description:
h2. Feature Description

This Feature delivers a production-ready, linux/amd64 container image of the example-voting-app vote service — a Python 3.12/Flask/gunicorn application — built from the existing vote/Dockerfile (final stage) with a one-line source patch to replace the hardcoded Redis hostname with an environment variable. The image is pushed to the Scotiabank Artifact Registry at us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/scotiapoc-migration-demo/migration-demo/example-voting-app:latest. The Feature covers the full build pipeline from source clone through image verification, including platform enforcement (linux/amd64), dependency pinning, and npm/pip vulnerability scanning. End users and platform engineers gain a repeatable, immutable artifact that can be deployed to any GKE namespace without CF buildpack tooling.

The vote service is a stateless Flask web app with two routes (GET / and POST /) that pushes voter choices as JSON to a Redis list. The container exposes port 80 and runs gunicorn with 4 workers. The only runtime dependency beyond the Python standard library is Redis connectivity — supplied via the REDIS_HOST environment variable injected at deploy time, not baked into the image.

h2. Business Value

Delivering a registry-resident container image decouples the vote service from PCF buildpack infrastructure, enabling the platform engineering team to deploy, rollback, and scale the service independently of any legacy platform tooling. Platform engineers gain a 10-minute build-to-registry cycle (vs 45-minute CF push-and-verify cycle), reducing deployment lead time by approximately 78%. The immutable image tag eliminates environment configuration drift — the same artifact is promoted from test to atlas-prod — directly reducing incident risk during the PCF cutover window.

h2. Scope

*Included:*
* Patch app.py line 24: Redis(host=os.getenv('REDIS_HOST', 'redis')) — single-line change
* Build vote/Dockerfile final stage with --platform linux/amd64 and --push to Artifact Registry
* Upgrade base image from python:3.11-slim to python:3.12-slim
* pip audit: scan requirements.txt (Flask, Redis, gunicorn) for HIGH/CRITICAL CVEs
* Image verification: docker manifest inspect confirms linux/amd64 digest

*Excluded:*
* result service Dockerfile (Node.js — separate Epic)
* worker service Dockerfile (.NET/Go — separate Epic)
* seed-data container (one-off, no production runtime requirement)
* Multi-arch builds (arm64 not required by GKE node pool)

h2. Functional Requirements

*Requirement 1:* The system shall build the vote service container image targeting linux/amd64 platform using docker buildx with --platform linux/amd64 flag, producing a valid OCI image with a non-zero layer digest.
*Requirement 2:* The system shall patch vote/app.py before building the image so that the Redis connection uses os.getenv('REDIS_HOST', 'redis') instead of the hardcoded string 'redis', enabling runtime configuration via Kubernetes Secret.
*Requirement 3:* The system shall push the built image to us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/scotiapoc-migration-demo/migration-demo/example-voting-app:latest and verify the push digest matches the local build digest.
*Requirement 4:* The system shall upgrade the Python base image from python:3.11-slim to python:3.12-slim in the Dockerfile before build, ensuring the production image does not ship end-of-life runtime components.
*Requirement 5:* The system shall run pip-audit against requirements.txt and block the build if any HIGH or CRITICAL severity CVE is found without an accepted risk waiver.

h2. Non-Functional Requirements

*NFR 1 — Performance:* The docker buildx build and push pipeline shall complete within 10 minutes on a standard developer workstation with cached base layers.
*NFR 2 — Security:* The container image shall not run as root (USER directive set to a non-root UID). No secrets or credentials shall be baked into any image layer. pip-audit must report zero HIGH/CRITICAL unmitigated CVEs.
*NFR 3 — Platform:* The image manifest shall declare linux/amd64 as the sole target platform. docker manifest inspect shall confirm amd64 digest before deploy proceeds.

h2. Technical Considerations

* docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --push -t <registry>/example-voting-app:latest ./vote --target final
* Base image upgrade: FROM python:3.11-slim AS base → FROM python:3.12-slim AS base
* Redis env-var patch: sed -i 's/Redis(host="redis"/Redis(host=os.getenv("REDIS_HOST", "redis")/g' vote/app.py
* Registry auth: gcloud auth configure-docker us-central1-docker.pkg.dev --quiet
* pip-audit: pip install pip-audit && pip-audit -r vote/requirements.txt

title: GKE Runtime Configuration, Helm Deployment, and Health Validation
description:
h2. Feature Description

This Feature delivers the complete Kubernetes runtime configuration and Helm-based deployment of the vote service to the atlas-prod GKE namespace. It covers Kubernetes Secret creation for Redis connection details (REDIS_HOST pointing to the bitnami Redis headless service), ConfigMap provisioning for non-sensitive environment variables (OPTION_A, OPTION_B, FLASK_ENV, REDIS_PORT), a self-contained Helm chart with Deployment, Service, HorizontalPodAutoscaler, PodMonitor, and Ingress templates, and Helm install/upgrade to atlas-prod. Health validation confirms 2/2 replicas reach Ready state, the Ingress obtains an IP address from the nginx controller, and GET / returns HTTP 200 within 3 seconds. The Feature closes when the vote service is reachable on the Ingress hostname and sustains load at 100 concurrent requests without pod restarts.

The vote service binds to the bitnami Redis StatefulSet already deployed in atlas-prod from the Wave 1 k8s-express-api-example migration. REDIS_HOST is set to redis-headless.atlas-prod.svc.cluster.local, which is the write-capable headless endpoint of the bitnami Redis release. No new Redis infrastructure is provisioned by this Feature — it reuses the existing cluster Redis.

h2. Business Value

Deploying via Helm gives platform engineers a versioned, rollback-capable deployment artefact. The HPA (minReplicas: 2, maxReplicas: 4, cpu: 70%) ensures the vote service can absorb traffic spikes during peak voting windows without manual intervention. Ingress with nginx provides a stable DNS hostname for the vote UI, replacing the CF route (voting-app.apps.internal.scotiabank.com) with a GKE-native route (voting-app.atlas-prod.local). The PodMonitor CRD enables Prometheus scraping for the oncall Grafana dashboard, providing visibility that was previously unavailable in CF.

h2. Scope

*Included:*
* Kubernetes Secret: vote-secret in atlas-prod with REDIS_HOST=redis-headless.atlas-prod.svc.cluster.local
* ConfigMap: vote-config with OPTION_A, OPTION_B, FLASK_ENV, REDIS_PORT
* Helm chart: output/helm/example-voting-app/ with Deployment, Service, HPA, Ingress, PodMonitor templates
* Helm install/upgrade: helm upgrade --install example-voting-app ./output/helm/example-voting-app -n atlas-prod --wait
* Health validation: 2/2 pods Ready, Ingress IP present, GET / returns 200

*Excluded:*
* Redis provisioning (already in atlas-prod from Wave 1)
* PostgreSQL (consumed by result/worker services — out of scope)
* TLS certificate provisioning (cert-manager not yet deployed in atlas-prod)
* result and worker service deployments (separate Epics)

h2. Functional Requirements

*Requirement 1:* The system shall create a Kubernetes Secret named vote-secret in the atlas-prod namespace containing REDIS_HOST set to redis-headless.atlas-prod.svc.cluster.local, so the vote service pod resolves Redis connectivity without any plaintext credential in the Deployment manifest.
*Requirement 2:* The system shall generate a Helm chart with a Deployment template that mounts the REDIS_HOST value from the vote-secret Secret and all non-sensitive env vars from the vote-config ConfigMap, with containerPort 80, readinessProbe and livenessProbe on GET / port 80, initialDelaySeconds 10.
*Requirement 3:* The system shall configure a HorizontalPodAutoscaler targeting the vote Deployment with minReplicas: 2, maxReplicas: 4, and cpu utilisation target of 70%, so the service scales automatically during traffic spikes.
*Requirement 4:* The system shall configure an Ingress resource with ingressClassName: nginx, host: voting-app.atlas-prod.local, routing all traffic to the vote service on port 80, and the Ingress ADDRESS field shall be populated within 120 seconds of deployment.
*Requirement 5:* The system shall verify deployment health by confirming 2/2 pods are in Ready state, pod restart count is 0, and GET / on the service cluster IP returns HTTP 200 within 3 seconds.

h2. Non-Functional Requirements

*NFR 1 — Performance:* The vote service shall respond to GET / within 3 seconds under 100 concurrent requests, as measured by the smoke test suite.
*NFR 2 — Availability:* The Deployment strategy shall use RollingUpdate with maxUnavailable: 0 and maxSurge: 1, ensuring zero downtime during image updates.
*NFR 3 — Resource Governance:* Each vote pod shall have resources.requests of 100m CPU and 128Mi memory, and resources.limits of 500m CPU and 256Mi memory, matching the CF manifest memory: 256M allocation.

h2. Technical Considerations

* Helm chart root: output/helm/example-voting-app/Chart.yaml, values.yaml, templates/
* containerPort: 80 — must NOT be 3000 (this is Python/Flask, not Node.js)
* Redis headless service: redis-headless.atlas-prod.svc.cluster.local (bitnami Helm release from Wave 1)
* PodMonitor CRD prerequisite: kubectl get crd podmonitors.monitoring.coreos.com — install if absent
* terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60 (from CF manifest timeout: 60)
* Ingress prerequisite: nginx-ingress-controller pods Running in ingress-nginx namespace
